title: Chapter 15: The Lark Sings
content: Twelve hours.

The number sat in Aline's chest like a stone dropped into still water, its ripples touching everything — her breath, her pulse, the way her fingers trembled as she pinned the butterfly brooch to the emerald fabric. The dress was new, nothing like the midnight blue Cass had first given her. This one caught the light like moss over stone, deep and alive, and the black butterfly at her shoulder seemed almost to breathe against the green.
"You should cancel." His voice came through the phone propped on her vanity, low and tight.

She met her own eyes in the mirror. The liner was sharp. The lips were pale. The woman staring back looked like someone who could walk into a room and own it, and Aline decided she would be that woman.
"If I hide, he wins." She pressed the pin through the fabric, felt it bite. "I'm not hiding."

Silence on the line. She could hear the ambient hum of the Nest — the distant drip of water from the underground lake, the soft static of a vinyl record spinning somewhere in the depths. Then a breath, long and controlled.
"Promise me the butterfly stays on."
"I already pinned it."
"Promise, Aline."
She closed her eyes. The dress smelled like new silk and something faintly chemical — sizing, maybe, or the memory of a factory floor far from anywhere that mattered. "I promise."
"I'll be watching. Every second."
She almost smiled. "I know."

She ended the call and stared at the brooch. Its wings caught the vanity light, iridescent black threads woven through with something that shifted blue and green when she moved. Cass had added something to it recently — she'd felt the weight change, a tiny new density near the body of the insect. She'd asked. He'd said it was a reinforcement of the signal amplifier. She'd believed him.
She believed him about most things now.

The Mercury Lounge was barely a venue — two hundred and fifty seats crammed into a basement on the Lower East Side, its walls stained with decades of sound and sweat and spilled drinks. Aline had played here before, years ago, when she'd been nobody with a guitar and a voice that people told her was "interesting" in the same tone they used to describe unusual vegetables.

Now the green room was suffocating with the sheer density of industry bodies who'd shown up. Every A&R rep in the city. Label lawyers. Streaming curators. Two journalists from Rolling Stone who'd been seated in the back corner, pretending they weren't taking notes. The Mercury had never hosted this many suits. The air smelled like expensive cologne layered over old beer and floor polish, and someone had placed a sad platter of vegetables next to a bottle of prosecco that nobody was touching because everyone was too busy performing the industry version of a mating dance.
Aline stood in the wings and looked out at the crowd through the gap in the curtain.
Front row, center. Mags.

Her mouth was a firm line, arms crossed over a leather jacket that probably cost more than Aline's rent used to. She looked like she was daring the entire room to disappoint her. When she caught Aline's eye, she didn't smile — she gave a single nod. *I'm here. You're not alone.*
Aline's throat tightened. She scanned further.
VIP section. Corner booth with the sightlines.
Marcus Webb.

He sat like a man who owned the building. Hands folded. Back straight. No drink, no phone, no companion — just him and that absolute stillness that passed for elegance in the circles he moved through. His eyes were on the stage. On the empty stage. On the space where she would stand, as if he were already measuring it, claiming it.
The cold that hit her had nothing to do with the venue's AC.

He knew she'd be here. Of course he knew. The showcase had been public — all of Mercury's listings were. And Marcus Webb didn't stumble into anything. He'd bought a ticket the way he bought companies: with precision and the absolute certainty that the thing he wanted would be his.
She pulled back from the curtain. Her hand went to the butterfly brooch — a reflex now, touching it the way other people touch a crucifix or a wedding ring.
*I'm not hiding.*
The stage manager appeared at her elbow. "Two minutes, Ms. Rivers."
The lights came up gold.

Not the harsh LED flood of an arena or the flat wash of a television studio — Mercury's lights were old, warm, incandescent, and they touched the stage like candlelight. Aline walked out in the emerald dress and the gold found every thread, turned her into something gilded and alive. The crowd's murmur died. Two hundred and fifty people went silent in a room this small, and the silence was a physical thing — a held breath.
She didn't sit at the piano.
She didn't pick up the guitar waiting on its stand.
She stepped to the center of the stage, to the single microphone, and she closed her eyes.

The song wasn't on the setlist. Her band — a pianist and a cellist, hired for the night — exchanged glances. She hadn't told them. She couldn't. The song had been born in the dark, in the half-world beneath the city, and she didn't have sheet music for it because she'd never written it down. She'd carried it in her body the way you carry a secret — in the sternum, in the soft tissue behind the ribs.
She sang.

*"I found you in the underneath,*
*where the light doesn't bother to reach.*
*I found you in the hollow space*
*where they said nothing could live.*
*And you were more real*
*than anything I met in the sun."*

The cello came in first — she heard the player catch the key from her voice, felt the low vibration of it in the floorboards through the soles of her shoes. The piano followed, soft as rain on water. And her voice —
Her voice filled the room like water fills a vessel.

She knew the simile. She'd used it before, in "The Hollow," the song that started all of this — *her voice fills the room like water fills a vessel* — except that had been an observation, distant and clinical, the way you'd describe a phenomenon you didn't understand. This was different. This time, she was the water. She was choosing to fill the space. She was choosing to be heard.

*"They called you phantom, called you ghost,*
*said you were the dark between the notes.*
*But I have touched your hands —*
*I have heard the static in your chest —*
*and you are more real*
*than anyone I met in the light."*

She sang about the underground lake. About vinyl crackle in the dark. About a man who lived in the walls of the internet because the world had given him no other place to exist. About being found by someone who couldn't show his face but showed her everything else — every scar, every line of code, every fractured piece of a soul assembled from the debris of a system designed to consume people like kindling.
She sang it all. And her voice never broke. Not once.
The last note hung in the air like smoke.
Silence.

Not the uncomfortable silence of a crowd uncertain how to react — the *sacred* silence of a room that has been taken somewhere and hasn't yet figured out how to come back. She could hear the hum of the old lighting rigs above her. She could hear her own blood. She could hear Mags doing that thing she did when she was fighting not to cry — a sharp, wet inhale through her nose.

Then someone in the back stood up. And the woman next to them. And then the whole room — a wave of bodies rising, and the sound that came with it wasn't polite applause. It was a roar. It shook the walls. Two hundred and fifty people in a basement on the Lower East Side generated a sound so enormous it felt like it would lift the ceiling off, and Aline stood in the center of it, emerald dress catching the light, black butterfly at her shoulder, and she *felt* it — every vibration, every clap, every open mouth — entering through her skin and settling somewhere deep in the place where the song had lived.
Mags was standing now, too. Clapping harder than anyone. And even from this distance, Aline could see the tears on her face.
The green room door burst open before Aline could even set down the water bottle she'd grabbed.

"That was INCREDIBLE." Mags crashed into her like a force of nature — leather and jasmine perfume and the sharp clean scent of her shampoo, her arms wrapping tight. Aline hugged her back and felt the butterfly brooch press against Mags's shoulder, felt the tiny hard shape of it digging into the leather, and wondered — absurdly, insanely — if Mags could feel the surveillance equipment inside it through the fabric. If she could sense the listening device, the tracking chip, the whole architecture of Cass's protection humming against her skin.
She pulled back. "I know it's mine. That's the point."

Mags looked at her. Really looked — past the stage makeup and the post-performance flush and the dress that cost more than a month of groceries. Searching for damage, probably. For cracks. For the invisible thumbprint of whatever man had turned her voice into a viral phenomenon overnight.
"That voice," Mags said, quieter now, "has always been yours. Whatever he did — whatever technology, whatever trick — that's not what they're hearing. They're hearing *you*."
Aline's eyes burned. "I know."
"Do you?"
"I wrote that song myself, Mags. In the dark. With nothing but a voice memo app and his stupid vinyl collection." She almost laughed. "It's the most honest thing I've ever written."
Mags studied her for another long moment, then nodded once — the same nod from the front row, heavy with things unsaid. She squeezed Aline's hand once and left.
The door had barely closed when Aline's phone buzzed.
She looked down.
Marcus Webb.
*Stunning. My offer still stands. But I should tell you — I've made a discovery about your producer. We should talk. Tomorrow. My office.*

The words sat on the screen, precise and clean, each one placed with the care of a surgeon laying out instruments. *Your producer.* Not "the anonymous producer." Not "whoever's behind the music." *Your* producer. As if Cass belonged to her. As if Marcus had already drawn a line connecting them, a line he was preparing to cut.
The bait dangled. It gleamed.

She should ignore it. She should screenshot it and send it to Cass immediately, let VERA trace the metadata, let the whole apparatus of the Nest swing into defensive mode. She should burn this message and never think about it again.
She pocketed the phone without answering.
The side door was heavy — old iron and wood, painted black so many times the paint had become a texture, almost alive. She pushed it open and stepped into the alley behind the Mercury.

The night air hit her like a slap. Cold, sharp, carrying the smell of exhaust and rain-wet asphalt and the ghosts of Chinese food from the restaurant next door. She breathed it in — filled her lungs with the unprocessed, unfiltered, un-digitized city, the raw data of the physical world, and for a moment she was just a woman in a green dress standing in an alley, and the universe had no opinions about her whatsoever.
Her phone buzzed.
Cass.
*You were beautiful. Come home.*

She smiled. A real smile — the kind that starts in the chest and works its way up, unstoppable. She could picture him in the Nest, surrounded by screens, the blue light painting his face, watching a grainy feed of the Mercury stage and believing, maybe for the first time, that the things he touched didn't automatically turn to ash.
She typed back: *On my way.*

For one perfect, crystalline moment, everything balanced on its edge. She had performed. She had won. The song she wrote in the dark had conquered a room full of people who'd come to dissect her, and the ovation was still echoing in her bones. She had Mags. She had Cass. She had both worlds — the light and the underneath — and they weren't at war. They were harmony.
She was about to put the phone away when the earpiece activated.

It was a tiny thing — she hadn't known it was there. He'd added it to the brooch without telling her, woven into the body of the butterfly, and the first she knew of it was the faint vibration against the cartilage of her ear, a sensation like a wasp landing, and then VERA's voice — calm, measured, annihilating.
"Aline."
She went still. The alley, the cold, the distant sound of traffic — all of it receded.
"Marcus Webb's trace program just completed a node verification."
She pressed her fingers to the brooch. As if she could reach through it, reach Cass, reach anyone who could change what was about to come out of VERA's mouth next.
"Estimated time to locate the Nest is now six hours."
Six.
"I haven't told him yet."
The smile fell off her face like something dropped from a great height. Her hand stayed on the brooch, pressing hard enough to feel the metal through the silk, pressing hard enough to hurt.

Six hours. The Nest — the underground lake, the vinyl collection, the waterfall of code and light and Cass's hands on the keyboards and Cass's mouth on hers and the whole architecture of safety built on the assumption that the dark could stay dark — six hours until Marcus Webb found the door and kicked it in.
And Marcus wanted to meet tomorrow.

She did the math. Six hours from now was four in the morning. Tomorrow was already collapsing into the trajectory of his plan. He wasn't just chasing them. He was herding them. The text, the concert, the invitation — all of it a corridor with walls closing in, designed to funnel her into his office precisely when the walls of the Nest came down.

The cold air bit her bare arms. The emerald dress, the black butterfly, the ovation still ringing in her ears — they belonged to a woman who had a future. A woman who'd just had the best night of her life.
She stared at the phone in her other hand. *You were beautiful. Come home.*
Home. Which would not be home in six hours.
The clock wasn't ticking anymore.
It was screaming.
